skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: Equipment including diving suits, wet suits, oxygen tanks etc. used to enable people to undertake underwater activities.
Items that have been through a manufacturing process. Use specific type where known.
Military provisions, traditionally of all types but now used specifically for projectiles and propellants such eg. bullets and gunpowder.
Weapons including portable firearms and large artillery pieces.
Military weapons, amunition and stores.
Equipment, including uniforms, small arms and field pieces, used by the armed forces.
Manufactured goods, including shells, dynamite etc., containing substances which can be made to explode.
Weapons used for cutting and thrusting, consisting of a blade and a hilt.
Materials used for the construction of buildings, vehicles or other objects.
Thin, rectangular tiles, usually made of wood, used for roofing.
Bed coverings, usually of woollen or similar natural or artificial fabrics.
Threads of material used to form cloth.
Citrus fruit of a distinctive yellow colour.
The edible product of a plant, usually consisting of the seed and its sweet, juicy envelope.

Roman-era pottery, principally made in areas from modern-day France and Germany, for food use, with a characteristic glossy red surface and which may be either decorated or plain, and often produced in quantity as an export cargo.
Ceramic vessels produced out of wet clay which is shaped ad dried or fired to harden. In some cases other materials like sand are added to the clay.
Any of the plants of the grass family, Graminae, cultivated for their edible seeds, especially corn, wheat etc.
A grain, obtained from the grass Oryza sativa, eaten as a staple in many parts of the world.
Pieces of metal money usually small in size and circular in design. Manufacture from a variety of metals including gold and silver but more recently manufactured from base metals and alloys.
Gold fashioned into coins for use as currency.
Any material, including gold, silver, copper, tin etc. which is ductile, malleable, usually lustrous and a good conductor of heat and electricity.
a hard metal used in a lot of alloys.
A fatty substance rendered from animal fat and used in the manufacture of soap, candles and for dressing leather.
Products derived from the carcasses or hides of animals. Includes bone and ivory products, blubber and grease but not meat.
Colour or dye, often in powder form, from natural mineral or vegetable sources (e.g. ochre) or created through chemical process (e.g. lamp black).
The blue dyestuff prepared from the leaves of the plant Isatis tinctoria, and sometimes used to discribe the plant itself. Later superseded by indigo.
Medium-sized animals domesticated for their wool, skin, meat and milk.
The young of the sheep.
Wood, cut and seasoned for use as a constructional material.
The powdery residue left after the combustion of substances, especially wood and paper.
A light, ductile metal, used in the manufacture of aircraft etc.
A sulphate of aluminium and potassium used in the dyeing process as a mordant to increase the intake of dye into a cloth.
Aluminium oxide.
Goods, including portable objects and furniture, commonly found in the home.
A body of wax or tallow, usually formed into a long, thin, cylindrical shape enclosing a wick and burnt to produce light.
Substances, including acids, alkalis etc. obtained by or used in a chemical process.
Commercially manufactured sodium carbonate used in glass and soap making.
Items made from cloth, wool etc used as a covering for the human body.

Clothes which are distinctive in cut, colour and fabric and worn by members of the armed forces, emergency services or civilian organizations to help to distinguish their members form other organizations.
A yellowish, tarnish-resistant precious metal used to make jewellery and coins.
Very fine particles of gold.
A fertilizer containing nitrogen, derived from sodium nitrate.
Sand consisting of a mineral common in igneous rocks.
A material in a granular form that is smaller than gravel and created by the erosion of rocks.
Naturally occuring substances eg. coal and salt, obtained by mining.
Powder mixed with water and applied as a paste for binding stones and bricks or for forming floors, walls etc.
Bells used either as a percussion instrument or for other purposes such as calling attention, marking time periods etc.
Large keyboard, percussion instruments in which strings are struck by hammers to produce notes.
Use for unspecified musical instruments.
Thick, black liquid distilled from bituminous coal, and containing many organic chemicals.
Non bituminous coal
A fuel derived by dry distillation from coal.
A mixture of coals that have been formed into briquettes or blocks. This fuel took its name from the fact that it was under patent in the late nineteenth century.
Hard, black, carbonized plant matter, usually found in seams below the earth's surface and used as a fuel and for the manufacture of gas.
Materials or substances, including petrol, firewood etc., used as a source of heat or power.
Powdered coal.
The wood from trees of the type Quercus.
Products manufactured from plant materials.
This is the top term for the thesaurus.
A living thing that is not an animal. Use for unspecified plants only. Use a more specific type where known.
Includes all trees, flowers, vegetables etc. and any products manufactured from them.
Ground oats used as food, often in the form of porridge.
Oil manufactured from fish.
Includes raw and processed.
Oil obtained from the flesh of the coconut and used in ointments, soaps etc.
An uncrystallized syrup, produced as part of the sugar refining process. Used as a food additive and in the production of rum.
Oil produced from the pulp of olives.
A sweet fluid substance, the nectar of flowers, gathered and worked up for food by insects such as bees.
A clear liquid that forms oceans, rivers etc.
A fluid substance. Use for unspecified liquids only, where known use more specific type.
Drinks containing alcohol, an intoxicating substance found in fermented or distilled liquids.
Oil derived from palms, used for a variety of purposes.
A sour tasting liquid, produced from wine and other liquors, used as a preservative, in pickling, or as a flavouring for food.
The hair from the coat of goats and sheep, made into yarn and used in the manufacture of woollen cloth.
Goods, particularly cloth and garments, manufactured from wool.
A cord manufactured by twisting strands of material, such as flax, cotton, nylon etc., together to form a strong line.
Originally used to describe very strong, often thick, rope made of metal or hemp. Now can be used for protective flexible tubing encasing wires such as telephone wire.
Thick ropes and rigging.
Thin rods or threads of metal formed by rolling or wire-drawing and used for a variety of purposes including electrical circuitry.
The pelts of beavers used for the manufacture of fur coats and hats.
Animal skins which have the outer pelt of fur on them.
A bitter beer brewed using charred or browned malt.
An alcoholic drink made by the fermentation of malt and flavoured with hops or other bitters.
The fruit of certain varieties of the plant Capsicum Annum eaten as vegetables.
Sweet red peppers.
Paper products intended for note-taking or correspondence, e.g. blank notebooks, diaries, writing paper, postcards
Includes all forms of paper and any products manufactured from it.
Clay used in the manufacture of fire bricks.
Fine grained earth which becomes more plastic when water is added. Mainly used in the manufacture of bricks, pottery and other ceramics.
A fertilizer containing nitrogen.
Fossilized animal dung, often ground into powder and used as fertilizer.
A chemical fertilizer made by treating powdered phosphate rock with sulphuric acid.
A mineral salt consisting of potassium chloride and mangensium sulphate, used as a fertilizer.
Substances, either natural or man-made, used to make soil more fertile.
Dung or compost, carried either as an export cargo or as waste to be dumped out at sea.
Includes chemical and natural fertilizers and waste products. use more specific term.
The edible seed of the palm tree.
A cereal, yellow in colour, the grains of which are embedded in a core known as the cob.
The hard, black, heavy wood of various tropical trees native to Africa and Asia, used in furniture manufacture and for making ornaments.
A herbaceous plant grown for its fibre and marijuana, a drug derivative. The fibres are used in the manufacture of rope.
A liquid derived from wood tar through a distillation process. Now used to describe timber preservative derived from coal tar.
Materials or goods which have been produced wholly or in part from oil, e.g. petroleum.
A volatile, colourless distillate of petroleum used as a solvent and paint thinner.
A flammable liquid that occurs naturally and is also now distilled.
A dark, viscous liquid, obtained from the ground, which is refined for use as a fuel for motor vehicles and in generators to produce light and heat, and as a solvent.
A dark, viscous substance obtained, from the distillation of wood tar or turpentine, and used as a sealant for caulking the seams of a ship or boat.
A solid material which is combustible distilled from petroleum.
Hydrocarbon secretion of a tree or bush, either naturally exuded or deliberately tapped from the tree, valued for its varied uses in chemicals and perfumery.
A smooth, flammable liquid, produced naturally or from plants or animals, and used for a variety of purposes.
A mixture of paraffins distilled from petroleum and used as a fuel and solvent.
A thick, viscous, black liquid obtained by the destructive distillation of coal or other organic substances used for preserving wood or rope and for covering road surfaces.
A colourless, thick sweet liquid used in the manufacture of chemicals.
Shaped material used for covering buildings, usually as a roofing material though can be used on walls or floors. Often of fired clay they can be of other materials particularly stone.
Edible fruit of the vine, which may be either red or white.
Rock or hard minerals used as a building material and for carving etc.
A form of granite found in Cornwall
A granular, crystalline, hard rock often used as a building material.
Boulders of unprocessed granite.
Small fragments of granite.
Blocks carved out of granite and used as a building material.
Any of a large group of cold blooded vertebrates who live exclusively in water. Also can refer to the flesh of fish which is eaten.
Salmon which has been pickled in vinegar or brine to preserve it.
Fish of the Salmonidae family, caught for food.
Small, marine crustaceans are used as food.
Aquatic invertebrate animals whose outer covering is a shell, often 'farmed' to be eaten. Includes molluscs and crustaceans.
Vehicles owned or used by the armed forces. Includes tanks, armoured vehicles as well as civilian vehicles in use by the military eg. ambulances.
Tracked, armoured fighting vehicles designed for front-line combat.
Machinery and parts for the manufacture or assembly of military tanks, i.e. armoured vehicles.
A staple foodstuff made with flour, water and usually yeast, which is kneaded and then baked. Can include unleavened (no yeast) varieties.
Any plants which are grown to be eaten. Includes parts of plants, especially roots and leaves.
The plant, Allium cepa, whose bulb is used, usually for culinary purposes.
A piece of military ordnance, of sufficient size to require mounting and used for firing projectiles, traditionally cannon balls but more recently high explosive shells.
A lustrous, malleable metal, which is resistant to corrosion. Tin is used in alloys such as pewter and used in the manufacture of cans for the storage of food.
Sheets of iron or steel coated with tin.
Instruments for determining position at sea, e.g. compasses, sextants.
The latex of plants tacky in its normal state and elastic after treatment. Used for a variety of purposes.
A fine, soft fibre produced by silkworms and used to make thread for textiles. Also the cloth made from this thread.
The seed of the flax plant used to produce oil and, in LINSEED CAKE, as fodder.
The seed of the flax plant pressed into cakes and used as cattle fodder.
Oil produced from the seed of the flax plant. Used in the production of paints, varnish etc.
Hard wood, reddish-brown in colour with a fine-grain often used in the manufacture of furniture.
Small pieces of wood, tipped with a chemical composition which ignites when rubbed against a rough or, if safety matches, prepared surface. Normally contained within a small box.
The dried leaves of the plants, Nicotiana tabacum or Nicotiana rustica, which are smoked (in pipes, cigars or cigarettes), chewed or taken as snuff for their relaxing properties.
The stalks of cereals separated from the grain br threshing and used as fodder, basket manufacture, thatch etc.
Corrosive chemical compounds, usually liquids, which are neutralized by alkalis.
Corrosive, dense, oily acid, usually in aqueous solution, used in the chemical industry.
The hides of animals, either untreated or tanned.
Animal skins, either raw or dressed which are used in the tanning industry to produce leather.
Usually distilled alcohollic drink but also used for liquids where a substance is in solution with alcohol.
Alcoholic drink distilled from wine or fermented fruit juice.
Woven or felted fabric used in the manufacture of garments, tablecloths, towels etc.
Soft, loose-textured, woollen or man-made fabric.
Cloth that has been woven in fixed lengths for sale.
Fine cotton fabric of a delicate weave.
A type of cotton cloth of a pale yellow colour.
Letters and parcels transported on behalf of the dispatchers by the postal services.
Written communiques, often of a military or governmental nature, requiring fast delivery.
Citrus fruit, usually green.
A cereal used to make spirits, beer and coarse bread.
Coiled substance, usually metal or plastic, used to absorb shocks or in timepieces since it can stretch and return to its original shape.
Three-dimensional artwork usually created out of stone, e.g. marble; metal, e.g. bronze; ceramics, e.g. terracotta, or other suitable materials.

Any unspecified items without utility or function and prized for personal or domestic decorative value. Examples might be unspecified metal objects from the Bronze Age or Victorian Staffordshire china dogs.
A magnetic metal found abundantly in certain ores and widely used for the manufacture of tools, machinery etc. Iron is also a chief component of steel.

Also spelt "oszemundt" and other variants. Low-grade cast iron in the form of balls or spheres, produced in small workshops and exported across the North Sea and Baltic region. Particularly associated with 16th century Sweden.
An unprocessed mineral, collected for the extraction of metals or other substances contained within the mineral.
An ore, such as stibnite, containing a high proportion of antimony.
Bricks capable of withstanding heat without burning. Used to line fireplaces, kilns etc.
Pipes manufactured from copper used for a variety of purposes, especially plumbing.
Hollow cylinders or tubes of ceramic, wood, plastic or metal used to convey liquids, gases etc. Used in a variety of industries.
Products manufactured from stone.
Powder made from particles of stone used in mining to make the coal dust in the air less combustible.
Cut or uncut stones, usually intended to be set into jewellery, and including precious gems, e.g. diamonds, sapphires, or semi-precious materials, e.g. quartz, agate, amber.
Savoury ingredients, such as herbs, used to add flavour to food.
The stigmas of the crocus, Crocus sativus, used as a flavouring and for colouring food.
Various flavoured aromatic and savoury substances, such as herbs, salt etc, used to impart flavour to food.
A pungent condiment manufactured from the dried berries of the plant Piper Nigrum. Use for cargoes of peppercorns or ground pepper.
A mineral, Sodium Chloride, obtained by mining or the evaporation of brine used as a seasoning and for preserving food.
Animal skins which have been treated by tanning or similar.
An odourless, tasteless powder obtained from cereals and potatoes, used for stiffening fabric.
A highly-prized, hard, white, fine-grained dentine constituting the main part of the teeth of walruses, hippopotami and elephants. Used in the manufacture of ornaments and piano keys.
The large, pointed teeth, made of ivory, which are specially developed to protrude from the mouth of elephants.
A brittle silvery metallic element used in the manufacture of many alloys.
Meat precooked and then sealed in cans made from steel, tin or aluminium plate to preserve it.
The flesh of animals, either raw or cooked, used as food.
People who have paid to travel on a vessel without having to work their passage.
People who leave their native country to settle in another, often for financial or religious reasons.
Stiff hairs used in the manufacture of brushes.
Hair from the Angora goat
Hair taken from animals and used in a variety of manufacturing processes.
An airlike, fluid hydrocarbon suitable for burning to produce light, heat or power.
Thin plates of slate, usually rectangular in shape, used as roofing tiles.
Iron, usually unworked, in the form of bars.
The excrement of seabirds and bats used as a fertilizer.
Personnel serving with one of the three armed forces - army, navy or air force.
Persons captured in land or naval engagements either being transported to prison out of their native country, sent for exchange, or being returned home.
Supplies including food, drink and equipment necessary for a journey.
An alcoholic beverage distilled from grain or malt and usually flavoured with juniper berries.
Clay used in the manufacture of fine ceramics especially porcelain.
An oil based derivative used as a fuel in diesel engines.
An alcoholic drink made from fermented apple juice.
People who are considered to be the property of someone else and who are bound to obedience.
People who have been convicted of criminal offences. Traditionally criminals, even those convicted of petty offences, were transported from Europe to penal colonies in the Americas and Australia.
Human beings. Use more specific type where known.
A heavy substance, usually gravel, sand or lead, placed in the bilge of a ship or boat to provide stability.
Buoys used to direct craft on water along the correct route or away from a hazard. Usually used in coastal areas.
Products manufactured from timber.
The heartwood of a leguminous tree from which an extract is obtained. The extract being used for dyeing.
Wood used to produce a dye.
The plant, Allium sativum, whose bulb is used for culinary purposes.
An alloy, principally of copper or tin, but which may also include zinc and/or lead.
A substance secreted by some plants which hardens upon drying but is soluble in water.
Aromatic vegetable substances, either in their natural form or ground, used to add flavour or scent to food.
Spice from the bark of an East Indian tree
Pipes manufactured from cast iron and used for a variety of purposes.
Pipes manufactured from iron and used for a variety of purposes.
A sparkling, white wine traditionally from the Champagne region of France. Now used, loosely, to refer to any sparkling white wine.
An alcoholic drink made from fermented grape juice (or occasionally other fruits).
The stem of the plant Zingiber officinale, with a characterisitc hot and peppery taste, used in cookery and medicine.
Oil used to lubricate moving parts, particularly in machinery.
A type of Red Wine from Bordeaux in France.
Receptacles, including boxes, barrels etc., designed to contain something.
A standardized rectangular steel container used for the transportation of goods by road rail and sea.
Goods, particularly cloth and garments, manufactured from wool.
The hair from the coat of goats and sheep, made into yarn and used in the manufacture of woollen cloth.
A hard metallic element used in steels and magnetic alloys. Compounds containing manganese are used in glass-making and other industrial processes.
Steel in the form of rails eg. for use in the construction of railways.
Hard semi-translucent ceramic material made out of kaolin, quartz and feldspar which is biscuit fired at around 1000 degrees Celsius and glaze fired at around 1300 degrees Celsius.
Flat, rectangular piece of material, traditionally of wood but now of any material, used in the construction industries.
Grain from a cereal of the genus hordeum, often used in the manufacture of alcoholic spirits.
An ore containing a high proportion of manganese.
An ore containing sufficient quantities of a mineral as to make its extraction profitable.
Fish of the family Clupeidae, common to the North Atlantic. They are caught for food.
Pieces of timber shaped for use in the construction of buildings, barrels etc.
Missiles, such as cannon balls and musket balls, designed to be discharged from a gun or cannon using an explosive propellant.
Goods and equipment used to supply a navy.
Plain, white, cotton cloth often unbleached.
An oil obtained by the distillation of wood and used as a solvent and paint thinner and in some medical preparations.
Fish of the Salmonidae family, caught for food.
Any of a large group of cold blooded vertebrates who live exclusively in water. Also can refer to the flesh of fish which is eaten.
Salmon which has been pickled in vinegar or brine to preserve it.
Stone which has been crushed for use in the construction industry.
Solid bars made from pure tin.
A long thin piece of wood,
Intended for personal decoration, objects made of precious, semi-precious or imitation metals and gemstones.
Small fish, common in European waters, caught for food.
Fruit which has been preserved by drying.
Dried or partially dried grapes eaten as a fruit.
Cotton which has not been processed.
Goods manufactured from processed cotton.
Seed of the cotton plant from which edible oil is extracted.
The white, fibrous substance surrounding the seeds of plants of the genus Gossypium, which can be spun and used in the manufacture of thread and cloth or used in its raw form as wadding or packing.
A light porous acid volcanic rock used for scouring and, in powdered form, as an abrasive and for polishing. Also used to make lightweight concrete.
A malleable water repellant substance created by animals or as a coating to parts of plants.
Metal spikes used for fixing one object to another.
Small fish, Sardina pilchardus, common to European waters and eaten as a food fish.
Alloys of iron which have great strength, malleability and which are able to be tempered to different degrees of hardness. Used for the manufacturwe of tools, machinery etc.
Steel which has been formed into sheets.
Fruit native to the countries bordering the Mediterranean Sea.
A climbing plant (Rubia tinctorum) which was formerly cultivated for the reddish-purple dye obtained from the roots.
Cylindrical, self-propelled missiles which travel underwater and explode on impact.
A strong unbleached cloth manuifactured from hemp, flax or similarly coarse yarn. Used mainly in the manufacture of sails, tents and for painting on.
Citrus fruit of a distinctive orange colour.
Dairy produce formed from the curds of milk, coagulated by rennet and pressed into a solid mass.
Dairy produce obtained by churning cream and used in cookery as a source of fat.
Products, such as cheese and butter, manufactured from milk.
A spheroid produced predominantly by birds, though also produced by some animals, which encases the young creature providing a place for it to develop.
Dried produce especially, hay, straw etc. used to feed cattle.
Cattle fodder or fertilizer resulting from the extracting of oil from seeds.
Long narrow pieces of wood used for a variety of purposes.
General merchandise and saleable commodities. Use more specific type where known.

Baggage, plate, money, clothes and other personal belongings of all kinds. Use only where the vessel is a cargo vessel conveying the goods of a person of consequence, e.g. a noble or diplomat, rather than for a general passenger vessel.
Use for cargoes which are not specified.
A red dye made from the dried bodies of the insect Dactylopius coccus.
Stone consisting of nearly pure silica, which can be used as a building material or knapped to produce flint objects.
Objects designed to be played with, often a model or miniature replica of something and especially for a child.

Inshore craft used for carrying troops and military equipment used either for beach landings or as a ferry; as cargo smaller landing craft could be carried aboard larger craft in the D-Day operations, for example.
Fibres obtained from plants and used in the manufacture of ropes, sacking, canvas etc.
Originally a metallic alloy of copper and tin or zinc, now used solely for an alloy of copper and zinc.
Conelike spikes of the hop plant used in the brewing industry to give a bitter flavour to beer and malt liquors.
17th to early 19th century muzzle-loaded long guns.
Matting manufactured using the fibres from the outer husks of coconuts.
Solid spherical objects, manufactured from stone or metal, fired from cannon.
Grains of cereal used as food.
Machinery, e.g. drive wheels, used in powering and excavating mines, i.e. mines for the extraction of minerals such as coal, lead or tin.

Machines for mechanical sewing, as opposed to sewing by hand. Victoriran and early 20th century examples are usually constructed of metal and operated by a hand wheel or treadle. Later examples may be electrically operated and/or have a plastic shell.
Apparatus used for applying a mechanical force, or to perform a particular function. Use more specific type where known.
Machinery including tractors, ploughs, harrows etc, which are used on farms.
A cereal of the genus Triticum, which can be ground to manufacture flour for bread and pasta.
Large blocks of timber used to support railway tracks.
Equipment and parts used in the manufacture of railway tracks, locomotives and rolling stock.

Protective equipment, usually made of metal, to protect parts of the body, e.g. helmets, breastplates, or full suits of armour, to protect troops or individuals in warfare. Most examples date from the Middle Ages, obsolete from the 17th century.
Line, cord or thread, especially that used for tying up parcels, etc. Also use for "twine", i.e. twisted thread, for the same purpose, and medieval equivalents, e.g. "packthread".
Rock containing a high proportion of calcium phosphate.
Long poles or spars, usually set vertically, on sailing boats and ships to support the sails.

Structures, traditonally consisting of a long shank with two arms at one end and a ring to attach to cable at the other, used to hold a ship in one place by mooring it to the bottom of the sea. Usually made of stone or metal.
Materials used to equip and fit out a ship. Includes masts, spars, rigging etc. Use only where evidence is less specific, otherwise use most relevant term.
Poles of timber, metal etc. used as masts, booms or yards on a vessel.
Porous black residue of partly burnt wood, bones etc. used as fuel or, in the form of charcoal pencils, as a tool for drawing.
The hard material, consisting mainly of calcium compounds, which makes up the skeleton of vertebrates. Used for ornaments or ground for use in the manufacture of bone china.
The powdery residue of burnt bones.
Ground and crushed bones used as a fertilizer.
The powdery residue resulting from the combustion of a substance eg. wood and used for various manufacturing processes.
Outer cases for ammunition shells, e.g. Howitzer shells; not "live" ammunition, i.e. either at the first stage of manufacture, or having been expended.
Oil obtained from the liver of the cod, providing a rich source of vitamins A and D.
The blue dyestuff prepared from the leaves of the plant Isatis tinctoria, and sometimes used to discribe the plant itself. Later superseded by indigo.
A white, caustic solid obtained by heating limestone and used in the preparation of mortar and cement.
Items of crockery and cutlery which are used whilst dining, either to eat with or for table decoration.
Tableware manufactured from any kind of ceramic and either carried as an export cargo or for the benefit of crew and/or passengers on board ship.
Metal knives, forks and spoons, carried either as an export cargo or for crew and/or passengers.
Articles, usually woven from coarse fibres or made from hard wearing materials (eg. rubber or cork) used to protect the floor or for lying, sitting or kneeling on.
Preparations for beautifying or enhancing the body, face, hair or skin, e.g. creams, and coloured "make-up" items, e.g. rouge, lipstick, etc.
Construction blocks manufactured using clay which is then fired or sun dried.
Fibres of wood reduced chemically or mechanically to pulp for use in the manufacture of paper.
Solid bars made from steel.
Wood which is used as fuel for fires.
A preparation made from the evaporated juice of the rhizome of the plant Glycyrrhiza glabra, used medicinally and as a sweet, usually in the form of a black, chewy substance.
Dried leaves from the plant Camellia sinensis, which is used to make a drink by infusing the leaves in boiling water.
Beams of wood, iron, steel etc., used in the construction industry.
Metal, usually gold and silver, in the form of ingots prior to being minted into coinage.
Metal, either precious or base, in the form of bars and coins. Use more specific type.
Solid bars or ingots of silver.
Coarse jute sacking
Goods, usually foodstuffs, which have been sealed inside canisters, usually made of tin or steel, to preserve them.
Meat precooked and then sealed in cans made from steel, tin or aluminium plate to preserve it.
Mackerel which has been salted to preserve it.
Fish of any kind preserved by salting, for example salted cod such as bacalhau or bacalao.
A soft, powder used in the manufacture of bread and foodstuffs, obtained by grinding wheat or other grain.
Iron in the form of rails eg. for use in the construction of railways.
Vehicles owned or used by the armed forces. Includes tanks, armoured vehicles as well as civilian vehicles in use by the military eg. ambulances.
Transportation, usually wheeled or tracked, used for the conveyance of goods or people.
Tracked, armoured fighting vehicles designed for front-line combat.

Inshore craft used for carrying troops and military equipment used either for beach landings or as a ferry; as cargo smaller landing craft could be carried aboard larger craft in the D-Day operations, for example.
An earth derived pigment.
Waste material.
Solid bars made from pure Copper metal.
A reddish metal used in alloys, coins and the construction and electronics industries.
Flat stones used to create flat surfaces, e.g. pavements.
The beans, or powder resulting from grinding the beans, of shrubs of the genus Coffea. The powder is used to make the drink Coffee.
Cut human hair, usually intended for wig-making.
The fine, keratinized, flexible filaments that grow from follicles beneath the surface of the skin in mammals.
Waste material resulting from industrial manufacturing processes.
Solid bars made from pure iron.
Tools and equipment used for agricultural purposes.
Manufactured objects and devices used to apply a force to an object or perform a particular mechanical function.
Wooden boxes or cases for carrying fruit and other similar goods.
Small hand-held firearms.
A hard aromatic seed, obtained from the fruit of an East Indian evergreen tree and used as a spice.
Explosive devices which produce spectacular visual effects when ignited.
A sulphuric acid compound.
Solid bars made from pure zinc.
A hard, lustrous metallic element obtained from various ores, including sphalerite, and used in roofing, alloys and for galvanizing iron.
Fortified wine produced on the island of Madeira.
Dried seedless grapes used in cookery.
Equipment used to catch fish
Personal apparatus for telling the time, worn either as jewellery or purely functional in nature, usually on the wrist or as a suspended fob.
Seed of the cotton plant from which edible oil is extracted.
Cotton which has not been processed.
Goods manufactured from processed cotton.
The white, fibrous substance surrounding the seeds of plants of the genus Gossypium, which can be spun and used in the manufacture of thread and cloth or used in its raw form as wadding or packing.
Sulphates, used in medicine and to colour inks.
Explosive devices, usually of metal, placed in the ground or floating in water with the purpose of endangering enemy personnel or shipping.
A fertilizer derived from nitrogen; also known as nitrate of ammonia.
Fragments of metal broken up and set aside for reprocessing.
Worked pieces of stone. Use more specific type of stone where known.
Carved stones used for marking a grave.
A natural compound used as in the building and medical industies.
Aquatic invertebrate animals whose outer covering is a shell, often 'farmed' to be eaten. Includes molluscs and crustaceans.
A mollusc usually used as a foodstuff.
Supplies owned by, or for the use of, the government.
An exotic hardwood with great strength and durability.
Two-wheeled vehicles for personal transport with pedals for propulsion by the rider.
Equine mammal used primarily as a beast of burden.
Use for cargoes of live animals only.
A substance designed to emit a sweet smell.
Pipes manufactured from concrete used in the construction industry for drainage etc.
Sulphate of Barium
Domesticated animals, derived from the wild boar, kept as a source of ham, bacon and pork products.
Porous black residue of partly burnt wood, bones etc. used as fuel or, in the form of charcoal pencils, as a tool for drawing.
A waste product from smelting metal.
A powder made from charcoal, sulphur and potassium nitrate and used as an explosive in fireworks, flintlock guns etc.
Unrefined oil used in the manufacture of petroleum.
Articles, particularly drinking vessels, manufactured from glass.
Oil obtained from the rendered blubber of whales, used as a fuel in old oil lamps.
Ore containing high levels of lead, eg. galena.
Heavy caterpillar tractors fitted with broad blades to the front for moving or levelling earth and obstacles.
Ore containing a high proportion of sulphur.
Potassium Carbonate obtained from the ashes of burnt plant matter and used as a fertilizer.
A fine grained rock which splits easily into plates. Used for the manufacture of roofing tiles etc.
Thin plates of slate, usually rectangular in shape, used as roofing tiles.
Sedimentary clay with smectite mineral properties, originally used for the cleansing ["fulling"] of cloth; in the 20th century it was used for a wider range of industrial applications.
Mineral ore comprising mainly aluminium ore and mined for aluminium extraction.
A fatty substance derived from the fat of animals used in cooking.
A preparation made from the evaporated juice of the rhizome of the plant Glycyrrhiza glabra, used medicinally and as a sweet, usually in the form of a black, chewy substance.
Carriages, locomotives and other vehicles used on a railway.
Unfermented wine.
Instruments for measuring and displaying time. Often manufactured as a piece of furniture e.g.a grandfather clock.
Timber from trees of the genus Juglans, used for the manufacture of furniture
Salts or organic derivatives of phosphoric acid, especially compounds of Calcium or Iron found in cereals and minerals.
Hard iron ores
Ore containing high proportions of Iron.
Thin sheets of material used for a variety of purposes such as writing on, painting on, or printing on.

Clothes which are distinctive in cut, colour and fabric and worn by members of the armed forces, emergency services or civilian organizations to help to distinguish their members form other organizations.
Merchandise wrapped in canvas and tightly bound with cords or hoops to form bales.
Dried flower buds, used as a pungent aromatic spice.
A viscous, bituminous pitch which can be used for surfacing roads.
Solid bars made from pure lead.
A soft, heavy, malleable metal, often obtained from the mineral galena. Traditionally used in the construction industry for roofing and plumbing.
Wood from the elm tree.
Various seaweeds which are burnt to obtain the salts contained in their ashes, which are used in the manufacture of soap and glass.
Originally gold coins issued in Florence, but later used for various coins issued on the continent. In modern Britain, the two shilling piece.
An amber like substance derived from pine or turpentine.
Ore containing a high proportion of zinc.
Pieces of material, usually decorated, used as standards, for signalling or for decoration. Often used as a symbol of a particular nation eg. the Union Flag of Great Britain.
A mineral of copper, iron and sulphur and is the main ore of copper.
The meat obtained from cattle.
Documents written on sheets of paper or similar, collected together and bound between protective covers.
Goods, usually foodstuffs, which have been sealed inside canisters, usually made of tin or steel, to preserve them.
Meat precooked and then sealed in cans made from steel, tin or aluminium plate to preserve it.
Blocks of cast iron as it appears when first removed from the smelting furnace.
Threads prepared for use in a manufacturing process such as spinning, weaving etc.
Clothing, cloth and woven fabric manufactured from either natural or artificial fibres.
Wool, textile, or synthetic floor covering material.
Stone composed mainly of calcium carbonate, used as a building material and in the production of cement.
Food, usually hay or straw, kept for the feeding of animals.
Partially decomposed granite used to produce a glaze for porcelain.
The round seeds of the plant Pisum sativum which grow in pods and are eaten as a vegetable.
A hoop or band of material put around a wheel to protect it and, when filled with air, to provide a more comfortable ride.
Writing implements; formerly quill pens made of bird feathers, and in more modern times fountain pens made of various materials with metal nibs, both used with ink to write.
Articles of value particularly those made with precious metals and precious stones, usually worn for adornment.
Powder produced by grinding cacao beans, used as the main ingredient for the drink Cocoa.
Narrow strips of wood used in the building industry.

Any form of arms or ammunition whether from a formal organized military context or as a general cargo. Use specific terms - e.g. swords, bombs, where known; otherwise use this term primarily in contexts where the arms are unspecified or ancient.
Holders for one or more candles, usually of metal or pewter.
Rolled iron or steel formed into rails.
The fibrous stalks of the plant, Linum usitatissimum, which can be processed and used to manufacture linen.
Small fragments of waterworn stone used in the construction industry.
Hair from the Angora goat
Hair taken from animals and used in a variety of manufacturing processes.
Stiff hairs used in the manufacture of brushes.

Fish which have been caught and kept alive. Fish were kept alive on ships in earlier centuries to ensure they reached their destination as fresh as possible. In more recent times exotic fish have been kept alive to sell as pets.
Large, brown, hard-shelled seeds of the palm tree Cocos nucifera, the flesh and liquid contained within the shell can be eaten.
Large fish, native to the North Atlantic and adjacents seas which has been fished since the Middle Ages.
Softwood obtained from trees of the genus Pinus, often used for the manufacture of furniture.
Material consisting of fragments of stone, sand, gravel, slag etc. used in the manufacture of concrete.
Paper currency. Bank notes may be of any denomination or value and of any nationality.
A sweet, crystalline subtance obtained from sugar beet or sugar cane and used in cooking, brewing etc.
An ornate, openwork fabric made by twisting, knotting or looping threads. Originally made by hand but more recently manufactured mechanically.
Frozen water. Prior to the invention of the refrigerator, ice was used to preserve food.
Goods, usually foodstuffs, preserved in glass bottles, jars, etc. Examples are bottled fruit, pickled onions, jam, honey.
A cloth woven from flax.
Vegetable matter decomposed in water and partially carbonized to form bogs, whence slabs are cut out to use as fuel.
The hard, close-grained wood of the Box or similar trees.
Large fish, native to the North Atlantic and adjacents seas which has been fished since the Middle Ages.
Tabelware manufactured from china clay.
Containers filled with high explosive, which can be fired from artillery pieces, thrown, dropped by aircraft or placed by hand and detonated in various ways.
Durable wood from the cedar tree.
Soft, pear-shaped fruits of the Ficus carica tree. They can be eaten fresh or dried.
A seed or fruit with a hard shell, often edible or used to produce oils.
A liquid used to provide a protective or decorative cover to a surface.
A grass, used in paper making, native to Spain and North Africa.
Items worn or carried as an adjunct to clothing, for example, headdresses, sashes, fans, masks etc.
A non-metallic element, yellow in colour, used in a variety of processes.
A mineral containing high quantities of Copper.
Equipment enabling a horse to be ridden or driven, eg. saddles, bridles, harnesses, stirrups etc.
"Looking glasses", up to and including the medieval period made of polished steel or other reflective metals; later a piece of glass with a reflective coating, e.g. silver or aluminium.
The top layer of earth consisting of humus and rock particles.
Sedimentary clay with smectite mineral properties, originally used for the cleansing ["fulling"] of cloth; in the 20th century it was used for a wider range of industrial applications.
Optical and/or navigational instruments with powerful lenses for observing remote objects or used in astronomy.
A calcareous, hard substance, the external skeleton of marine polyps, used in the manufacture of jewellery.
A strong, sweet fortified wine made in Portugal.
A liquid distilled from petroleum.
Ceramic vessels made from potash, sand, feldspar and clay which is biscuit fired at a temperature of around 1000 degree Celsius. Usually glazed in order to become watertight.
The cured flesh of a pig.
Canvas or similar fabric used for the manufacture of sails.
Fish eggs, e.g. from cod or, for example, caviar.
Ore that has been burnt.
Railway engines designed to travel under its own power and to draw railway carriages and rolling stock.
Fertile soil composed mainly of humus, sand and clay.
Metal hoops used to hold the staves of barrels in position.
Containers of cylindrical form, traditionally made of curved wooden staves bound together by hoops of metal. Also includes modern plastic and metal barrels.
The round, firm fleshed fruit of the apple (Malus Domestica) tree. Grown in numerous varieties throughout the world.
Ore containing a high proportion of tin.
Traditionally any food preserved by boiling with sugar, salting, pickling etc. Nowadays used specifically for fruit which has been preserved with sugar.
Usually slag, a residue from burning stone, but can be used for vitrified bricks.
Movable, usually functional, articles normally found in the home.
Carriages, locomotives and other vehicles used on a railway.
Goods, including machinery, manufactured from iron.
Planks or boards manufactured from softwood timber.
Calcium sulphate used as a fertilizer and for making plaster of Paris.
Large mammals of the genus Bos, domesticated to provide milk, beef and materials eg. leather. Use only for live animals.
The outer layer of tissue on a tree or woody plant, often used in the dyeing and tanning industries.
One of the allotropes of carbon, commonly used in pencils.
Draught animals produced by mating asses and horses.

Articles intended for personal adornment, usually to be strung on items of jewellery or sewn onto clothing. They may be made out of worked raw materials (eg. shells) or be manufactured goods, for example glass beads.
Wood which is soft or easily cut, particularly the wood of coniferous trees.
An alcoholic spirit distilled from sugar products, especially molasses.
A powder made by grinding the edible part of various grains, and used as a foodstuff.
Timber sawn into long, flat, pieces used for flooring etc. in buildings.
Pieces of wood used to form the sides of barrels.
Poles of timber, metal etc. used as masts, booms or yards on a vessel.
Potassium nitrate
Dried kernel of coconut
Small seeds of the leguminous plant Lens culinaris, eaten in stews and soups.
A dark, viscous liquid, obtained from the ground, which is refined for use as a fuel for motor vehicles and in generators to produce light and heat, and as a solvent.
Dried grass used as fodder.
Salt in its natural state
A semi metal, poisonous element used in a variety of industrial processes and in some medicinal preparations.
Small fish, common in European waters, caught for food.
A fibrous silicate mineral used to manufacture incombustible items.
Building material made by combining gravel, cement, water and sand. When hard it forms a stonelike surface, used for paving and building.
Collective name, in a true geological sense, for a metamorphosed limestone eg. Carrara marble. In British sense, any decorative stone that will take a polish, eg. Purbeck marble.
Blocks cut from marble.

Any form of arms or ammunition whether from a formal organized military context or as a general cargo. Use specific terms - e.g. swords, bombs, where known; otherwise use this term primarily in contexts where the arms are unspecified or ancient.
Ammunition manufactured for use in hand held firearms.
Wood from non-coniferous trees, eg. oak, known for their hardness.
A light, buoyant material obtained from the cork oak. Cork is used in floats and as stoppers for bottles.
Narrow necked containers, originally of leather but now usually made of glass or plastic. Used for storing liquids.
Bells used either as a percussion instrument or for other purposes such as calling attention, marking time periods etc.
Machinery and equipment used in the manufacture of aircraft.

Aircraft as cargo carried whole or crated ready for assembly on arrival at the destination port. Do not use where the vessel is an aircraft carrier and the aircraft is carried as part of the vessel's armament.
Seeds of any of the various related plants of the leguminous plant Vicia faba.
A fine white clay used specifically for the manufacture of clay tobacco pipes and for whitening leather.
A cleansing agent, manufactured by reacting natural oils and fats with a strong alkali. Nowadays, formed into bars with added colouring and perfume.
Planks or boards manufactured from softwood timber.
Paste or cake of the fruit of the cacao-tree, sweetened with other substances and used to make beverages or confectionery.
Fish which has been preserved by drying.
Fish of any kind preserved by salting, for example salted cod such as bacalhau or bacalao.
Mackerel which has been salted to preserve it.
Traditionally, pieces of unleavened cake or bread usually small in size and dry, crisp and hard.
Large mammals, domesticated for riding and as draught animals.
Sodium carbonate, usually derived from common salt, used for a variety of purposes including the manufacture of glass and soap.
Dried or partially dried plums eaten as fruit.
The seed of a cereal plant used in the manufacture of foodstuffs.
The edible tubers of plants of the family Ipomoea Batatas eaten as a vegetable.
Silver fashioned into coin for use as currency.
Coal dust or slack, especially that of anthracite.
A mollusc usually used as a foodstuff.
Fish of the family Clupeidae, common to the North Atlantic. They are caught for food.

Any animal other than livestock (animals carried as food); animals used for transport (horses; mules); and domestic pets. Such animals may be exported as specimens, for zoos, menageries, and circuses, or as unusual pets (e.g. elephants, monkeys).
A sulphuric acid compound.
Collective name for the seeds of various cereal grasses.
Goods stowed in barrels and therefore indicating that the barrels were in use as containers with goods stored therein, rather than as cargo in their own right.
Fish which has been preserved by drying.
A metallic alloy of iron, carbon and silicon formed by casting in a mould.
Stones used to edge a pavement or raised path.
Fat from an animal used to lubricate objects.
A pure starch obtained from the tubers of the plant Maranta arundinacea used in cookery as a thickening agent.
An earth derived pigment.
Seed of the cotton plant from which edible oil is extracted.
The grains of various plants from which new plants grown.
Timber manufactured from fir or spruce trees.
Steel, usually unworked, in the form of bars.
The flat end of a barrel.
Small fish, Sardina pilchardus, common to European waters and eaten as a food fish.
A high explosive, manufactured by absorbing unstable nitroglycerine in a material such as kieselguhr, a soft, earthy material.
Traditionally a mineral that could be used for striking fire but now a term commonly used to refer to various sulphides, especially iron disulphide.
A substance used to affect a human or animal by changing mental or physical states, particularly used in medicine.
A solid material which is combustible distilled from petroleum.
A heavy, metallic element, which is liquid at room temperature. Used in thermometers and medicinal preparations.
An alcoholic spirit distilled from malted barley or barley and maize or rye.
Mud, which has often been dredged from the beds of rivers.
The oil rich seed of the plant Bracchus napus.
A sulphide of copper and iron, reddish brown in colour.
Waste matter, usually liquid in form, consisting of the contents of sewers and cesspits.
The edible seed of the palm tree.
Grain, especially barley, prepared for brewing, distilling or vinegar making.
A compound consisting of potassium and silica, used in various processes.
A cable laid under the surface of the sea, often on the seabed, for various purposes including telecommunications.
Product of boiled blubber
The bark and wood of trees of the genus Aspidosperma used to produce tannin.
People who serve as members of an army.
Hard, transparent, brittle substance manufactured by fusing soda or potash with other ingredients. Used for glazing windows and in the manufacture of drinking vessels.
A three dimensional representation of a figure, whether animal or human, produced by carving, casting sculpting or similar.
Fragments of wrought or cast iron broken up for reprocessing.
Soft, porous objects used in bathing and for cleaning. Originally made from the dried skeletons of marine sponges but now more commonly manufactured from synthetic foam.
A form of limonite, found in wet ground, from which iron can be extracted.
A dark blue powder, obtained from plants or synthetically manufactured, used as a dye.
A precious metal, lustrous white in colour. Used for the manufacture of coins and other valuable items.
Small stones which have been rounded usually by the action of sea water.
The hard, heavy wood obtained from plants of the genus Guaiacum.
A series of links joined together to form something that is flexible and strong. Usually metal, chains have a variety of uses but usually involve joining one item to another.
A plant, Salsola soda, burnt to produce alkali.
An alloy, traditionally of tin and lead but now of tin, antimony and copper, used for domestic utensils.
Fabric manufactured from fibrous materials, consolidated by heat and compression so that the fibres become matted together.
A large block of wood used to support the roof of a mine.
Small, marine crustaceans are used as food.
Soft, damp soil.
The flesh obtained from pigs eaten as food.
The bony outgrowths found on the heads of mammals used for display or combat used for a variety of purposes.
Animals and products made from the carcasses and hides of dead animals.
Small fragments of wood or bark.
Sheets of iron or steel bent into a series of ridges and hollows to give added strength and rigidity.
Soft limestone consisting almost entirely of calcite.
Bone like material which grows in a branch-like fashion on the heads of male deer and reindeers in general.
A white powder used to colour medicines, paints etc.

Fish which have been caught and kept alive. Fish were kept alive on ships in earlier centuries to ensure they reached their destination as fresh as possible. In more recent times exotic fish have been kept alive to sell as pets.

Double-handled storage jars characterised by a long neck and long body used as food and wine containers and for carrying such goods as cargo, found on shipwrecks from Classical antiquity (Greek/Roman).
Machinery and parts for the manufacture or assembly of military tanks, i.e. armoured vehicles.
Cargoes transported in sacks or bags, usually wheat, corn, flour, fruit, or coal. To be used only where the cargo is said to be "sacks" or "bags" of goods. Use specific term where known.
The fat removed from the carcass of a whale or seal and used in the manufacture of cosmetics and as a fuel for tallow lamps.
The seeds of the palm Phytelephas macrocarpa, the albumen of which, hardens resembling ivory in colour, texture and toughness and is used to make ornamental buttons etc.

Protective equipment, usually made of metal, to protect parts of the body, e.g. helmets, breastplates, or full suits of armour, to protect troops or individuals in warfare. Most examples date from the Middle Ages, obsolete from the 17th century.
Zinc, now mainly used for smelted zinc but has been used to include zinc ores.
A sweet fluid substance, the nectar of flowers, gathered and worked up for food by insects such as bees.
